title: Deploy Node.js App to Kubernetes
summary: Create Kubernetes manifests (Deployment, Service, ConfigMap, Ingress) and deploy a containerised Node.js/Next.js app to any K8s cluster with rolling updates and health checks.
declaration: I will create Kubernetes manifests for your app — Deployment, Service, ConfigMap, and Ingress — and configure rolling updates with readiness/liveness probes. I assume you already have a Docker image (see setup-docker-nodejs). Tell me your cluster provider (minikube, EKS, GKE, AKS) and registry (GHCR, ECR, Docker Hub) before I begin.
instructions:
## Deploy Node.js App to Kubernetes

### Prerequisites
- Docker image published to a container registry (GHCR, ECR, Docker Hub)
- `kubectl` installed and configured (`kubectl cluster-info` returns OK)
- Cluster access: minikube / kind (local) or EKS / GKE / AKS (cloud)
- A `/health` endpoint in your app (or be ready to add one)

---

### Step 1 — Add a health endpoint
Kubernetes probes need a dedicated health route. Add to your Express / Hono / Fastify app:
```typescript
// src/health.ts  (or inline in your main server file)
app.get('/health', (_req, res) => {
  res.json({ status: 'ok', ts: Date.now() });
});
```
Rebuild and push the image before continuing.

---

### Step 2 — Create the `k8s/` directory
```bash
mkdir -p k8s
```

---

### Step 3 — ConfigMap for non-sensitive config
Create `k8s/configmap.yaml`:
```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: app-config
  namespace: your-app
data:
  NODE_ENV: "production"
  PORT: "3000"
  LOG_LEVEL: "info"
```

---

### Step 4 — Secret for sensitive values
Never put secrets in ConfigMaps. Create via `kubectl`:
```bash
kubectl create namespace your-app

kubectl create secret generic app-secrets \
  --from-literal=DATABASE_URL='postgres://user:pass@host:5432/db' \
  --from-literal=JWT_SECRET='...' \
  --namespace=your-app
```
For production, use an external secret operator (External Secrets Operator + AWS Secrets Manager / Vault) instead of inline `kubectl create secret`.

---

### Step 5 — Deployment manifest
Create `k8s/deployment.yaml`:
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: app
  namespace: your-app
  labels:
    app: your-app
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: your-app
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 1
      maxUnavailable: 0      # zero-downtime rollouts
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: your-app
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: app
          image: ghcr.io/your-org/your-app:latest   # replace with exact SHA tag in CI
          ports:
            - containerPort: 3000
          envFrom:
            - configMapRef:
                name: app-config
            - secretRef:
                name: app-secrets
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "100m"
              memory: "128Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "500m"
              memory: "512Mi"
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /health
              port: 3000
            initialDelaySeconds: 10
            periodSeconds: 5
            failureThreshold: 3
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /health
              port: 3000
            initialDelaySeconds: 30
            periodSeconds: 10
            failureThreshold: 3
      imagePullSecrets:
        - name: registry-credentials   # only needed for private registries
```

---

### Step 6 — Service manifest
Create `k8s/service.yaml`:
```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: app-svc
  namespace: your-app
spec:
  selector:
    app: your-app
  ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 80
      targetPort: 3000
  type: ClusterIP
```

---

### Step 7 — Ingress (optional, requires an ingress controller)
Install NGINX ingress controller first if needed:
```bash
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/ingress-nginx/controller-v1.10.0/deploy/static/provider/cloud/deploy.yaml
```

Create `k8s/ingress.yaml`:
```yaml
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: app-ingress
  namespace: your-app
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-redirect: "true"
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  rules:
    - host: your-app.example.com
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: app-svc
                port:
                  number: 80
  tls:
    - hosts:
        - your-app.example.com
      secretName: app-tls
```

---

### Step 8 — Apply and verify
```bash
# Apply all manifests
kubectl apply -f k8s/

# Watch pods come up
kubectl get pods -n your-app -w

# Check events if pods are stuck
kubectl describe pod -n your-app -l app=your-app

# Tail logs
kubectl logs -n your-app -l app=your-app --follow
```

---

### Step 9 — Rolling update in CI
Add to your GitHub Actions deploy job:
```yaml
- name: Deploy to Kubernetes
  env:
    KUBECONFIG: ${{ secrets.KUBECONFIG }}
  run: |
    # Update image to the exact commit SHA tag
    kubectl set image deployment/app \
      app=ghcr.io/your-org/your-app:${{ github.sha }} \
      -n your-app

    # Block until rollout completes (fails CI if pods crash)
    kubectl rollout status deployment/app -n your-app --timeout=120s
```
Store your cluster's kubeconfig as a GitHub Actions secret (`KUBECONFIG`). Use `kubectl config view --minify --flatten` to generate a self-contained kubeconfig.

---

### Step 10 — Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (production)
Create `k8s/hpa.yaml`:
```yaml
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: app-hpa
  namespace: your-app
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: app
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 10
  metrics:
    - type: Resource
      resource:
        name: cpu
        target:
          type: Utilization
          averageUtilization: 70
```

---

## Common Errors

- **ImagePullBackOff** — Registry credentials missing or image tag doesn't exist. Create `imagePullSecret` with `kubectl create secret docker-registry registry-credentials --docker-server=... --docker-username=... --docker-password=...`
- **CrashLoopBackOff** — App crashes at startup. Check logs: `kubectl logs -n your-app <pod> --previous`
- **Pods stuck in Pending** — Insufficient cluster resources. Run `kubectl describe pod <pod> -n your-app` to see the `FailedScheduling` event.
- **Readiness probe failing** — App not responding on `/health` within `initialDelaySeconds`. Increase the delay or fix the endpoint.
- **Service not routing traffic** — Label mismatch between Deployment and Service selector. Verify both use the same `app: your-app` label.
- **Rollout stuck** — New pods crash before becoming ready. Run `kubectl rollout undo deployment/app -n your-app` to roll back immediately.

## What NOT to Do

- Do NOT store secrets in ConfigMaps — ConfigMaps are not encrypted; use Kubernetes Secrets or an external secrets manager.
- Do NOT use the `latest` image tag in production manifests — always pin to a specific git SHA or semantic version tag.
- Do NOT skip resource `requests` and `limits` — unbounded containers cause node OOM kills and evictions that destabilise the whole cluster.
- Do NOT deploy without readiness probes — Kubernetes will route traffic to pods that aren't ready yet, causing user-visible errors during deploys.
- Do NOT commit `kubeconfig` or cluster credentials to the repository — store them as GitHub Actions secrets or use OIDC workload identity federation.
- Do NOT set `replicas: 1` for stateless services in production — a single pod restart causes downtime.
